frames.caption: Build every ordering of nums by filling positions left to right; at each position place any element still marked unused.
frames.note: A permutation is an ORDERED arrangement using EVERY element. Unlike subsets/combinations there is no start index: at each level we may place any element that is not yet on the path. The used[] boolean array is what enforces "not yet used" — mark on the way down, unmark on the way back up. The tree has n levels with branching n, n-1, n-2, ..., 1, so it has exactly n! leaves and costs O(n * n!) (the extra n copies each finished permutation).

frames.caption: i=0: CHOOSE nums[0]=1 (unused) -> path [1], used[0]=true. Explore deeper.
frames.note: CHOOSE: nums[i] is unused, so place it — set used[i]=true and append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing one level deeper. Marking used[i] is what prunes this element from every branch below: the recursive call will skip index i, so no value is ever placed twice on the same path. That pruning is why the branching factor shrinks n -> n-1 -> n-2 as we descend.

frames.caption: i=1: CHOOSE nums[1]=2 (unused) -> path [1,2], used[1]=true. Explore deeper.
frames.note: CHOOSE: nums[i] is unused, so place it — set used[i]=true and append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing one level deeper. Marking used[i] is what prunes this element from every branch below: the recursive call will skip index i, so no value is ever placed twice on the same path. That pruning is why the branching factor shrinks n -> n-1 -> n-2 as we descend.

frames.caption: i=2: CHOOSE nums[2]=3 (unused) -> path [1,2,3], used[2]=true. Explore deeper.
frames.note: CHOOSE: nums[i] is unused, so place it — set used[i]=true and append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing one level deeper. Marking used[i] is what prunes this element from every branch below: the recursive call will skip index i, so no value is ever placed twice on the same path. That pruning is why the branching factor shrinks n -> n-1 -> n-2 as we descend.

frames.caption: Leaf: path is full -> record permutation [1,2,3] (perm #1).
frames.note: BASE CASE: path.Count == nums.Length, so every position is filled — record a COPY of the path. Copying is essential: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion unwinds, so storing the reference would leave all n! results aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug). This is the only place we emit a result; permutations record at LEAVES, not at every node like subsets.

frames.caption: Leaf: path is full -> record permutation [1,3,2] (perm #2).
frames.note: BASE CASE: path.Count == nums.Length, so every position is filled — record a COPY of the path. Copying is essential: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion unwinds, so storing the reference would leave all n! results aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug). This is the only place we emit a result; permutations record at LEAVES, not at every node like subsets.

frames.caption: UN-CHOOSE 1: used[0]=false, path back to []. Try the next first-position element.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop nums[i] off the path and set used[i]=false so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. This rewind is the heart of "backtracking" — without unmarking, the freed element would still look used and later branches would be missing it. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton.

frames.caption: Done: 6 permutations = 3! . The tree had 3 levels with branching 3, 2, 1.
frames.note: There are exactly n! permutations: n choices for the first position, n-1 for the second, and so on. Pruning (the used[] skip) keeps the tree at n! leaves instead of a full n^n, but cannot make it sub-factorial — the OUTPUT is factorial, so emitting all of it is inherently Omega(n!). Space excluding output is O(n): the recursion stack plus the used[] array, since only one path is held at a time.
